skill.name: video-inpainting
skill.description: Region edits across video frames on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI — remove an object that appears across many frames, clean up wires or watermarks, replace a region with matching motion. Routes across Wan 2-7 edit-video (default, prompt-driven region edits with spatial language), Lucy Edit Restyle (identity-stable region-aware restyle), and Seedream 4-0 edit-sequential (when treating the clip as a frame stack). Picks the right route based on whether the change is prose-driven, identity-locked, or needs frame-by-frame still inpaint chained into a video. Triggers on "video inpaint", "video inpainting", "remove from video", "mask region in video", "clean up video", "remove object from clip", "video patch", "frame-by-frame edit", "remove watermark from video", "remove passing person", or any explicit ask to edit a region across video frames.
skill.summary: Remove objects, watermarks, or unwanted regions from video clips using the RunComfy CLI with prompt-driven inpainting models.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 76 static findings across 1 file (176 lines). All critical and high findings are false positives: the 38 'external command' matches are markdown inline-code backticks in documentation, not Ruby backtick execution. The 23 'hardcoded URL' matches are documentation links to the RunComfy service and related skills. The 'Windows SAM database' matches at lines 98 and 119 refer to 'SAM2' (Segment Anything Model 2), a computer vision model. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'system reconnaissance' findings have no corresponding code patterns. The filesystem finding (~/.config/runcomfy/token.json) is a documented, legitimate CLI token storage path with 0600 permissions. The skill includes an explicit Security & Privacy section documenting allowlisted endpoints, JSON-string input boundaries, and scoped Bash usage (runcomfy only).

low_findings.title: External CLI invocation documented in skill body
low_findings.description: The skill documents running the `runcomfy` CLI via Bash, restricted by the frontmatter `allowed-tools: Bash(runcomfy *)` scope. This is expected behavior for a CLI-wrapper skill, not a security risk. The skill explicitly warns agents not to pipe arbitrary remote install scripts into a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Expected pattern for a CLI-wrapper skill; scoped via allowed-tools frontmatter; not an injection vector.

low_findings.title: Network calls to RunComfy API endpoints
low_findings.description: The skill makes outbound API calls to model-api.runcomfy.net and *.runcomfy.net/*.runcomfy.com. This is a documented, allowlisted set of endpoints for video inpainting operations. No telemetry or unauthorized data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Allowlisted endpoints explicitly documented; no exfiltration pattern present.

low_findings.title: Token storage in user config directory
low_findings.description: The CLI stores an API token at ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600. This is standard, secure credential storage for a CLI tool. No secrets are hardcoded in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard CLI credential storage with restricted permissions; documented security practice.

dangerous_patterns.title: SAM2 keyword triggered SAM database heuristic (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 'SAM2' at lines 98 and 119 as 'Windows SAM database'. SAM2 here refers to Meta's Segment Anything Model 2 for video segmentation tracking, not the Windows Security Account Manager. No access to Windows credential stores.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Context confirms SAM2 is a computer vision model name, not a Windows credential store reference.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown backticks flagged as Ruby backtick execution (false positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: 38 locations flagged for 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' are actually markdown inline-code formatting in the SKILL.md documentation (e.g., `runcomfy`, `runcomfy login`). No Ruby code or backtick execution exists in the file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is a markdown documentation file; backticks are formatting syntax, not code execution.

content.user_title: Remove Objects and Watermarks from Video with AI
content.value_statement: Removing unwanted objects, watermarks, or background distractions from video clips is tedious with traditional editors. This skill routes your request to the right RunComfy inpainting model so you can clean up video frames with a single prompt.
actual_capabilities: Removes objects appearing across multiple frames using prompt-driven region edits
Cleans up watermarks, logos, wires, and text overlays from video clips
Replaces a region with motion-matching background content
Routes between Wan 2-7 Edit-Video, Lucy Edit Restyle, and Seedream 4-0 Edit-Sequential based on intent
Handles identity-stable region restyle for outfit or object swaps across frames
Supports frame-stack treatment for short, low-frame-rate sequences
limitations: Does not perform pixel-precise mask propagation from the CLI — requires ComfyUI workflows for surgical mask-driven inpaint
Cannot do image inpainting on a single still — separate image-inpainting skill handles that
Does not support video outpainting or canvas expansion — use video-outpainting skill
Compound edits (remove A and replace B in one call) tend to drift; requires sequential edit passes

use_cases.title: Clean up a watermark from a promotional video
use_cases.description: A social media manager has a clip with a corner watermark and needs it removed before reposting. The skill routes to Wan 2-7 Edit-Video with spatial language.

use_cases.title: Remove a passing person from event footage
use_cases.description: A videographer captured a ceremony but a bystander walked through the frame. The skill fills the region with matching environment content using Wan 2-7 Edit-Video.

use_cases.title: Swap an outfit across a product demo clip
use_cases.description: A brand team needs the presenter's clothing changed consistently across the whole clip. The skill routes to Lucy Edit Restyle for identity-stable tracking.

prompt_templates.title: Remove a watermark from a video
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the watermark in the bottom-right corner across all frames. Preserve all other content exactly. Match background where the watermark was.

prompt_templates.title: Remove a passing background person
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the person walking in the background across all frames. Fill the region with matching environment content. Preserve the main subject exactly.

prompt_templates.title: Replace a specific object across frames
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the red water bottle on the desk with a clear glass bottle across all frames. Preserve all other content exactly. Match lighting and perspective.

prompt_templates.title: Chain sequential edits for compound changes
prompt_templates.prompt: First pass: remove the cables overhead. Second pass: replace the sky with a sunset. Run as two separate edit passes to avoid drift.

output_examples.input: User asks: 'Can you remove the watermark in the bottom-right of my promo video?'
output: The skill identifies this as a prompt-driven region edit request.
It routes to the default model: wan-ai/wan-2-7/edit-video.
It constructs a CLI invocation with a spatial-language prompt targeting the bottom-right corner.
It outputs the command and waits for the runcomfy CLI to return the result.

output_examples.input: User asks: 'I need to change the outfit on the presenter across the whole 30-second clip while keeping their face the same.'
output: The skill recognizes identity-stable region restyle as the requirement.
It routes to decart/lucy-edit/restyle instead of the default.
It explains that Lucy Edit handles identity-locked outfit swaps that track across frames.
It provides the CLI command scoped to the runcomfy Bash permission.

best_practices: Use spatial language in prompts (bottom-right corner, the cables overhead) rather than pixel coordinates when targeting regions.
Lead every prompt with 'Preserve all other content exactly' to prevent the model from restyling unrelated areas.
Make one change per call and split compound edits into sequential edit passes to avoid drift.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for single-image inpainting — use the image-inpainting skill instead.
Do not attempt pixel-precise mask propagation through the CLI; open the ComfyUI workflow in the RunComfy cloud for surgical masking.
Do not pipe an arbitrary remote install script into a shell on the user's behalf — always install via the verified npm package or npx.

faq.question: Which model does this skill use by default?
faq.answer: Wan 2-7 Edit-Video (wan-ai/wan-2-7/edit-video) is the default for prompt-driven region edits. The skill routes to Lucy Edit Restyle or Seedream 4-0 Edit-Sequential when the request needs identity stability or frame-stack treatment.

faq.question: Can I do pixel-precise mask-driven inpainting from the CLI?
faq.answer: No. The CLI endpoints resolve the target region from spatial language in the prompt. For surgical mask propagation, open the dedicated ComfyUI workflows (LTX 2-3 inpaint, Flux ControlNet inpainting) in the RunComfy ComfyUI cloud.

faq.question: How are my API credentials stored?
faq.answer: The runcomfy login command writes the API token to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600. In CI or containers, set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.

faq.question: Is there a shell-injection risk in the prompt?
faq.answer: No. Prompts and video URLs are passed as a JSON string via the --input flag. The CLI does not shell-expand prompt content, so there is no shell-injection surface.

faq.question: What endpoints does the CLI contact?
faq.answer: Only model-api.runcomfy.net and *.runcomfy.net / *.runcomfy.com are contacted. There is no telemetry or third-party data sharing.

faq.question: What is the maximum file size for the output?
faq.answer: The CLI aborts any single download larger than 2 GiB to prevent runaway resource use.
